artifacts.content:
Hi Rach,

I’m hiding in the Willow Creek office between sessions with a lukewarm oat latte, so you’re getting the brain-dump version.

I was thinking about you this morning because one of my supervisees brought a vicarious trauma case, and it pulled me right back to our 2018 stretch. I still remember logging that 6‑month supervision engagement with you (April–September 2018) for the Oxford Brookes diploma – more than 40 hours total – and how often the Harborview removal on June 4, 2018 showed up. The way your panic attacks would spike just walking into the child-protection wing… I’m grateful you let me be in that with you, even though in hindsight it was a messy dual-role experiment.

That whole February 2018–February 2019 period feels like a blur now: shuttling between my NW 23rd office for sessions with Alan, the residentials in Oxford, and late-night FaceTimes with you in Seattle after another horrific CPS shift. It’s wild that those supervision logs – including our work together – are now just bullet points in my CV, when for you it was about surviving.

On the very mundane front: Ellie has her robotics competition scrimmage on Saturday, Jake has yet another birthday party (trampoline park this time), and I’m trying to figure out when to drive down to Eugene next to help Mom and Dad clear out the basement. Mom swears she’s “just organizing,” but I can hear the downsizing anxiety between the lines.

How are you sleeping these days? Any fewer Harborview nightmares? Text me if email feels too heavy.

Love,
M

Hi Alan,

I’m finally sitting down to do my quarterly reflection and realized how often our 2015–2016 work on NW 23rd still echoes in my supervision practice.

I was talking with a peer yesterday about countertransference, and I found myself describing those late 2015 sessions with you, when I was so flooded after Daniel’s written warning on October 9, 2015 for mishandling PHI in that product demo at his healthcare software company. I can still picture your office – that armchair by the window – and how we spent multiple hours between January 2015 and March 2016 unpacking my anxiety and shame about being “the ethics person” at work while my own partner was under formal scrutiny.

Those 40 hours I logged with you ended up as neat evidence in my April 2016 EMCC Senior Practitioner portfolio, but what stays with me is how you normalized the messiness: that my fear about Daniel losing his job bled into how I held clients who were also under investigation or performance review. It’s helped this year with a Portland biotech leader I’m supervising another coach around – he’s under an informal SEC look for insider-trading rumors, and I can feel the old “fix it, keep everyone safe” part of me lighting up.

Life outside the consulting bubble: Ellie is knee-deep in middle school friendship drama, Jake just discovered Dungeons & Dragons, and my mom managed to fall in her Eugene driveway in November (she’s okay, just bruised and cranky). Daniel and I are still debating whether he should stay in product or find something with fewer landmines.

Hope you’re well and still walking that ridiculous hill up from NW 23rd.

Warmly,
Melissa

metadata.subject: How our 2015 work is still shaping me

artifacts.content:
Event: Trauma-Informed Coaching & Supervision w/ Dr. Karen Mills (online)
Date/Time: March 13–15, 2020, 9:00 a.m.–3:30 p.m. PT daily
Location: Home office – Willow Creek Ave, Suite 304 (Zoom)
Attendees: Melissa Hart + cohort (Trauma Recovery & Resilience Center)

Notes:
- Registered after supervision call re: Providence St. Vincent Medical Center exec client who disclosed active opioid relapse post–back surgery. Realized my 2018–2019 Oxford Brookes supervision training didn’t fully equip me for intersection of addiction, medical trauma, and performance management.
- Intention: strengthen trauma-informed lens for both direct coaching and supervision of internal coaches in healthcare systems.

Prep:
- Re-read supervision notes from last three sessions with Providence SV client; highlight where I felt out of depth.
- Revisit Hawkins & Shohet 7-Eyed Model + my own boundaries around what is “coach/supervisor” vs “clinician.”
- Block out kids’ schedules: Daniel on point for school runs + meals those three days.

Follow-up tasks (post-intensive):
- Bring case to monthly peer supervision circle (Priya, Mark, etc.) – especially the ethical tension of holding relapse information when I’m supervising another coach in the same hospital.
- Update supervision contracting language for healthcare clients around disclosures of substance use and safety.
- Consider weaving learnings into Friday morning writing block for the ethics-in-supervision book project.

Reminder: Be gentle with nervous system. This will likely stir up old material from my own December 2011 collapse and early 2012 therapy with Dr. Mills at Portland Insight, plus echoes of Rachel’s Harborview trauma work.

metadata.title: Trauma-Informed Coaching & Supervision Intensive
metadata.location: Home office – 2157 Willow Creek Avenue, Suite 304, Portland, OR

artifacts.content:
Title: Supervision practice timeline + money/health overlay
Location: Willow Creek office – whiteboard transcription
Date: 2025-11-03

I keep telling people I’ve been supervising for “about seven years,” but the texture of those years matters.

Oct 2018–Feb 2019: early practicum. Friday morning supervision blocks at 2157 Willow Creek Ave, Suite 304, stacked carefully around Jake’s OT appointments for his sensory processing diagnosis. Couldn’t do late afternoons – he melted down after school. At the same time, Ellie was in and out of Randall Children’s for generalized anxiety evals. Bandwidth razor-thin.

2019–2021: supervision 6–8 supervisees max because we were still sitting on ~$40k in medical + legal bills from Jake’s complicated birth and the custody mess with Daniel’s sister. My Oxford Brookes supervisor gently suggested cutting executive coaching hours; reality said “no” until that debt dropped.

Spring 2020: three-month pause on new supervisees when West Hills Charter went fully online and Ellie’s panic attacks escalated. Kept just three supervisees, all on Zoom from the home office, so I could hover near her telehealth CBT sessions with Dr. Karen Phillips at Providence Behavioral Health.

Jan 2020 onward: peer supervision circle (Priya in London, Mark in Chicago, others) – 2 hours/month on Zoom. March 18 and July 22, 2021 sessions stand out: we sat with my supervision dilemmas around the Portland biotech SVP under an informal SEC inquiry for insider-trading rumors. My old reactivity from Daniel’s 2015 PHI incident lit up.

Note to self: this is the messy, embodied context underneath the clean “10–14 supervision hours/month” line in my marketing copy.

metadata.title: Supervision timeline + life load
metadata.location: 2157 Willow Creek Avenue, Suite 304, Portland, OR

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Quick update while I wait for Jake to finish homework (we’re doing the 15-minute bursts his teacher suggested; it’s actually working with the ADHD).

You asked the other day when I “really” started this coaching thing. The honest answer is that the seeds were way back at Northwest Health Systems – Spring 2009-ish – when Elena Ruiz, my manager, was on that quiet performance improvement plan and had weekly check-ins with HR downtown. She’d come into my office beforehand and we’d basically do coaching: rehearsing conversations, grounding her anxiety, clarifying what she wanted to ask for. We never called it that, but in hindsight it was my first internal client.

From there it snowballed: those March 2010 burnout debriefs with Dr. Patel’s oncology nurses after the critical incident at the Portland campus, then the January 2010 mentor-coach role becoming semi-official. By June 2012 I was taking on paid clients on evenings and Saturdays – partly to chip away at the $68k in loans from PSU and Pepperdine and the $14.5k Columbia tuition, partly to cover the $650/month therapy with Dr. Karen Mills at Portland Insight after my December 2011 collapse.

Remember when we almost fell two months behind on the Washington Park mortgage in 2019 after Daniel’s bonus got cut? That’s when I ramped up again, which is part of why the practice looks so “established” now.

Anyway, that’s probably more history than you wanted when you just asked how long I’ve been doing this. Kids are fine, Daniel’s buried in another release, and I’m planning to come down to Eugene next Sunday to help you and Dad with the attic boxes.

Love you,
Mel

metadata.subject: When this coaching thing really started

artifacts.content:
Hey Priya (cc: Mark),

I wanted to give you both a bit more context before Thursday’s peer supervision circle so I don’t have to info-dump at the start.

Case 1 is the Portland biotech SVP I mentioned last month – the one who disclosed there’s an informal SEC inquiry into alleged insider trading. I’m supervising another coach who works with his team, and I’m wrestling with how much of that confidential disclosure I can ethically hold while still supporting her. It keeps echoing Daniel’s October 9, 2015 written PHI warning at his healthcare software company and the way my own anxiety hijacked sessions back then.

Case 2 is from my Team Coaching International days (May–Oct 2019): cross-functional leadership team at a Portland health insurer. One VP, whom I’d coached individually in 2017, quietly reminded me of his history of major depressive episodes and a suicide attempt in college at Oregon State. That material resurfaced when I was later supervising another coach on the same team. I actually brought that case to Tatiana Bachkirova’s “Ethics and Power in Coaching Supervision” intensive on March 11–12, 2021, but it’s still alive for me.

Overlay all of this with the Providence St. Vincent exec who disclosed an active opioid relapse after back surgery – the one that pushed me into Karen Mills’ trauma-informed supervision intensive at the Trauma Recovery & Resilience Center (March 13–15, 2020) – and I can feel my system tightening around “safety” and disclosure.

On the lighter side: I finally blocked Friday mornings again for the ethics-in-supervision book, Ellie’s robotics team is obsessed with their claw design, and Jake is lobbying hard for a dog.

See you Thursday on Zoom.

M

metadata.subject: Set-up for Thursday’s circle – SEC, OSU case, opioid relapse client

artifacts.content:
Title: Coaching origin stories – which one do I tell?
Date: 2025-06-19
Location: Sellwood café (solo morning)

I keep noticing how many different “start dates” I carry for my coaching life, depending on the audience.

1. Professional narrative: June 2012 – first paid client at Providence St. Vincent while still in HR. This is the clean version. I say I’ve been coaching ~13.5 years.

2. Financial survival story: early 2012 – taking on side clients to cover the $650/month therapy with Dr. Karen Mills at Portland Insight after the December 2011 collapse, plus the $68k MA/EdD loans and $14.5k Columbia tuition. Coaching as debt service.

3. Family crisis arc: March 2015 – FMLA when Ellie was hospitalized at Randall Children’s for severe anxiety and panic attacks tied to bullying at West Sylvan. I never went back to the old HR job; I poured everything into Hart Leadership & Coaching, LLC.

4. Quiet, messy beginnings: Fall 2008 at South Eugene High, doing monthly stress-management sessions with two of Dad’s colleagues who were having panic attacks in their classrooms and flirting with medical leave. Or Spring 2009, helping Elena Ruiz prep for her weekly PIP check-ins with HR at Northwest Health Systems.

5. Community story: June–December 2013, our Hillsdale living room group for three laid-off PMs from Daniel’s healthcare software company, every other Tuesday night.

Maybe the truth is: I’ve been coaching as long as I’ve been sitting with people in crisis and asking, “What do you need right now?” The credentials and dates (Oxford Brookes 2018–2019, EMCC 2016, TCI 2019) are the scaffolding, not the beginning.
